coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical and spectroscopic evaluation of 6-MP and its interaction with carbon dots and dsDNA
coredata.dc:creator: Ipte, Priyanka R.
Manna, Sudipa
Satpati, Ashis Kumar
coredata.dc:description: The 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP) is an anti-cancer drug used for the treatment of acute lymphocytic leukaemia. Electrochemical signal of 6-MP over the unmodified Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) is poor and not suitable to probe the molecule through electrochemical measurements. In order to enhance the electrochemical signal from the molecule, the substrate has been fabricated by electrodepositing gold nano particles (AuNPs) on the surface of GCE. The electrochemical signal of 6-MP has been enhanced significantly over the modified substrate. The peak current of 6-MP obtained as a result of the electrochemical oxidation increases with subsequent addition of carbon nano dots (CD) in the solution, which revealed the strong interaction between the 6-MP with CD. The interaction between 6-MP and CD is established using UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The intensity of fluorescence of CD is quenched on interaction with 6-MP in the solution, strong quenching constant value beyond the diffusion-controlled limit, indicates stronger interaction between 6-MP and CD. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) investigations imaged the change of the texture of dsDNA upon its interaction with 6-MP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 6-Mercaptopurin (6-MP)
Carbon dots (CD)
AuNPs
Voltammetry
Biosensor
Drug-DNA interaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigations of interaction between 6-MP and CD
Spectroscopic investigation of 6-MP and its interaction with CD
Fluorescence spectroscopy for interaction between 6-MP and CD

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors thank Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC) for fully funding of the project. Authors also thank Dr. A. K. Tyagi, Director, Chemistry Group and Dr. C. N. Patra, Head, Analytical Chemistry Division for the encouragements and supports in the project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Current normalised DPV of 0.1 mM 6-MP with varying pH values (B) Corresponding plot of the peak potential vs the pH of the solution. DPV plot of (C) 3 mM 6-MP with varying concentration of CD (D) corresponding plot of peak current vs CD concentration, (E) DPV plot of 6-MP using AuNPs modified GCE with the variable concentration and in presence of 0.01 µM of CD at pH 7.4 (F) Corresponding calibration plot of peak current vs the concentration of 6-MP.

(A) Particle size distribution of CD (B) The TEM image of CD drop casted using dilute CD containing solution (C) Zeta potential analysis of pure 6-MP, CD and 6-MP-CD complex (D) FTIR spectrum of CD in presence of 6-MP at different concentrations.

(A) UV–vis absorbance spectra of 6-MP with variable concentration and (B) Corresponding OD vs concentration of 6-MP (C) UV–vis absorbance spectra of CD with variable concentration (D) UV–vis absorbance spectra of 0.001 µM CD in presence of variable concentration of 6-MP (E) corresponding double reciprocal plot.
(A) DPV plot 0.0005 mM 6-MP with variable concentration of dsDNA (B) corresponding ΔE1/2 vs log [dsDNA] (C) UV–vis absorbance of 0.2x10- 4 M 6-MP in presence of variable concentration of dsDNA.
AFM images of (A) Blank silanized mica sheets (B) Control, untreated 100 µM dsDNA,100 µM dsDNA treated with 6-MP varying in concentrations from (C) 0.1 mM (D) 0.5 mM (E) 1 mM (F) 5 mM.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical and spectroscopic evaluation of 6-MP and its interaction with carbon dots and dsDNA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai 400085, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Centre, Trombay, Mumbai 400085, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Homi Bhabha National Institute, Anushaktinagar, Mumbai 400094, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Homi Bhabha National Institute, Anushaktinagar, Mumbai 400094, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Equal contribution from both the authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP) is an anti-cancer drug used for the treatment of acute lymphocytic leukaemia. Electrochemical signal of 6-MP over the unmodified Glassy carbon electrode (GCE) is poor and not suitable to probe the molecule through electrochemical measurements. In order to enhance the electrochemical signal from the molecule, the substrate has been fabricated by electrodepositing gold nano particles (AuNPs) on the surface of GCE. The electrochemical signal of 6-MP has been enhanced significantly over the modified substrate. The peak current of 6-MP obtained as a result of the electrochemical oxidation increases with subsequent addition of carbon nano dots (CD) in the solution, which revealed the strong interaction between the 6-MP with CD. The interaction between 6-MP and CD is established using UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. The intensity of fluorescence of CD is quenched on interaction with 6-MP in the solution, strong quenching constant value beyond the diffusion-controlled limit, indicates stronger interaction between 6-MP and CD. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) investigations imaged the change of the texture of dsDNA upon its interaction with 6-MP.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: 6-Mercaptopurine (6-MP) is an immunosuppressive and anti-neoplastic, thiopurine used as an anti-cancer drug to treat acute lymphocytic leukaemia [1,2]. The analytical determination of the drugs and investigation of its various interaction in various stages of developments is essential during the drug discovery and deployment. Development of analytical methods should be suitable for the determination at wide ranges of concentrations and the samples which may involve the real pathology samples (blood, serum, plasma, urine or any cellular component), effluent samples, discharged from the pharmaceutical companies and hospitals effluent from the cancer patient.[3–6].

The analytical methods which are used for the detection of 6-MP, which includes electrochemical methods [7], chemiluminescence [8,9], capillary electrophoresis [10], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11], mass spectrometry [12] and Raman assays [13]. Among the above listed methods, electrochemical methods are easy to operate and provides accurate results of such drugs and biomolecules.[14–18] Additionally, for onsite detection of drugs, it is necessary to develop a sensor which can be easily transported and work with optimum maintenance, electrochemical methods are best suited for the purpose. [19–22].

The conventional electrodes are not suitable to generate good electrochemical signal from 6-MP, the modification of electrodes are required and modifications with the modifiers like CNT and TiO2 are reported to generate good electrochemical signals suitable to propose the analytical methods [2,23,24] It has been reported that in chemotherapy treatment for cancer the 6-MP works as anti-metabolites having strong resemblance with the metabolites naturally present in the body, but they differ enough in their functional properties which interrupts normal cellular metabolic processes.[25] When a cell incorporates these anti-metabolite substances instead of its reassembling natural component, the reaction product of these interactions disturbs its normal functioning, which may halt the cell division and may ultimately lead to cell death. 6-MP is known as a purine antagonist [26] and having influence in the pathway for the synthesis of purine ribonucleotide [27,28]. Thus 6-MP and its different reaction by-products work differently with the cancer cells, among them the interaction with DNA of the cellular system is one of the pathways responsible for controlling the growth of cancer cells. [29–32] In view of this, special focus has been made to probe the interaction of 6-MP with the dsDNA, in addition to the development of analytical methods of its determination.

As the substrate of choice to modify the electrode it has been reported that the gold nano particles (AuNPs) have special affinity towards 6-MP and used as carrier media of the drug [27,28]. Similarly, carbon nano tubes and nano particles are also utilised as a carrier for several drugs [33–42].The favourable interaction between the 6-MP and the AuNPs are utilised in the modification of electrodes by AuNPs for the enhancement of the electrochemical signal from 6-MP and the interaction of 6-MP with carbon nano dots (CD) have been investigated. Electrochemical techniques along with spectroscopic measurements provide excellent platform in the determination of the drugs and the evaluation of drug biomolecule interactions [43–51].

Herein the electrochemical investigation of 6-MP over the AuNPs modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) has been investigated. The AuNPs/GCE thus fabricated was used as a sensor of 6-MP. The interaction of 6-MP with carbon dots (CD) and dsDNA was carried out using the fabricated AuNPs modified GC electrode substrate. The interaction of the drug with CD has evolved with interesting observation of generation of enhanced current signal, the enhancement in the signal has been argued with the possibilities of enhancement of the mass transport or through the activation of the AuNPs/GCE substrates by the presence of CD. The entanglement of 6-MP with dsDNA has been evaluated using the electrochemical investigations and the deformation pattern of dsDNA has been imaged using atomic force microscope (AFM).

6-MP was procured from Otto Chemie Pvt. ltd. with 99 % purity, which was used for experiments as obtained without further purification. The calf thymus dsDNA was purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used as received. Ethylene diamine (purity ≥ 99 %) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used for CD synthesis. The citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O) used as a precursor for the synthesis of CD, was procured from Chemco Fine Chemicals, Mumbai having 99.7 % purity. Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO) used as organic solvent, procured from J. T. Baker Chemical Co., Phillipsburg. The salt of Auric Chloride (AuCl3) with 96 % purity and KNO3 with ≥ 99 % purity was obtained from Sigma Aldrich and used as obtained for the electrochemical deposition. The ultra-pure water from Milli-Q system (17.9 M ohm) was used throughout the study. Stainless steel autoclave with inner Teflon liner having total volume capacity of 100 mL was used for hydrothermal synthesis of CD. Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4) and potassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4) were bought from Sarabhai M Chemicals to prepare phosphate buffer. Ortho phosphoric acid(H3PO4) and potassium hydroxide (KOH) purchased from Merck, were used to control pH. Phosphate buffer of pH 7.4 was used for the electrochemical measurements.

All the electrochemical experiments were carried out on CHI 920D potentiostat using 3 electrode system. AuNPs modified GCE with 3 mm diameter, platinum (Pt) wire with the surface area of 1 cm2 and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode respectively. UV–Visible Spectroscopic determinations were carried out using Ocean Optics QE 65,000 spectrophotometer with spectrasuit software. The dip probe assembly from Ocean Optics having path length of 1 cm was used for the absorption probe. Fluorescence Spectroscopic Analysis was carried out using Optosolar CWS 300 model connected with a monochromatic and an in-house fabricated fluorescence measurement cell. Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) from Nanosurf with C-3000 controller having Tap 190 A1-G silicon tip was used for morphology characterization. The hot air oven from Meta Lab with model number MSI-12 was used for the hydrothermal synthesis of CD at 200° C. For measuring and adjusting pH of various solutions used in the experiments the digital pH analyser model ELICO LI 614 was used. The FTIR instrument model Tensor II from Bruker and the zeta potential measurement system model Litesizer 500 from Anton Paar were used for the IR spectroscopy and the zeta potential measurements respectively. The time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) instrument from IBH was used for the fluorescence life time measurements of CDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out using the Phillips-CM 200 electron microscope, operated at 200 kV.

The CD was synthesized using hydrothermal method where 1 g citric acid and 320 µL ethylene diamine were added with 60 mL ultra-pure water. The mixture was stirred well and poured in stainless steel hydrothermal chamber (Autoclave) having inner Teflon liner of 100 mL capacity. The hydrothermal chamber was incubated at 200° C for 24 h. After completion of the reaction, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature and subjected to centrifugation process at 15,000 rpm at 25 °C for 30 min. The supernatant consisting of CD was collected and used for further experimental procedure. The concentration of the CDs is determined from the measured absorbance by correlating it with the molar extinction coefficient of CDs [52–54]. The molar extinction coefficient of CDs is reported to be 1 × 106 M−1cm−1 at 340 nm.

The GCE was cleaned by polishing it on polishing cloth dusted with 0.05 µm sized alumina powder on it, then washed thoroughly with ultra-pure water and finally air dried. The cleaned GCE was electrodeposited by AuNPs from 1 mM Auric Chloride in 0.1 M KNO3 solution. The electrodeposition of AuNPs on the surface of GCE was achieved through electrolysis by applying −0.3 V potential under stirring condition for 200 s. The AuNPs modified GC electrode thus formed was used as an electrochemical sensor for the investigation and detection of anticancer drug 6-MP in aqueous solution. The memory effect from the subsequent electrochemical scans is significantly lower. In case of any enhancement in the current with respect to the prolonged dipping time inside the 6-MP solution, the electrodes are recommended to be kept in 1 M KOH solution for 10 s and subsequent washing with deionized water.

The electrochemically deposited AuNPs on the surface of GCE were characterized by using AFM imaging as a characterization tool. Figure S1 of supporting information shows AFM images of synthesised AuNPs. The gold nano particles are observed to be deposited over the GCE surface with near spherical shape, all the nano particles are well distributed over the GCE. The AFM investigations reveals that the average diameter of each AuNPs of ∼ 105 nm.

The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) experiments were conducted for the detection of 6-MP from the solution with the experimental parameters as increment potential (step potential) 4 mV, Pulse amplitude 50 mV and pulse width 0.5 s. Commercially available GCE is unable to generate good electrochemical response from the oxidation of the drug. Electrochemically deposited AuNPs modified GCE was used as a working electrode for the detection of 6-MP. As seen from Fig. 1 , the oxidation of 6-MP provides an electrochemical oxidation peak at 0.49 V at neutral pH. With variation in the pH of the solution, the peak potential varied substantially, which indicate that electron transfer process has strong influence with the prototropic conditions of the medium. As seen from Fig. 1A, the peak potential was shifted towards less positive direction with increase in pH of the solution. The peak potential of 6-MP was plotted with pH of the solution and the correlation generated straight line relation with slope ∼ 53 mV/pH (cf. Fig. 1B). The slope of the plot indicates the involvement of equal numbers of protons and electrons during the electrochemical oxidation process of 6-MP. Further measurements are carried out in phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigations of interaction between 6-MP and CD
ce:section.ce:para: The CD was synthesised to investigate its interaction with 6-MP. The interaction has been investigated through the DPV measurements where the electrochemical signal of 3 mM 6-MP was monitored over the AuNPs modified electrode surface with the addition of CD in the test solution, corresponding results are shown in Fig. 1C. The oxidation peak of 6-MP was observed to be increased with increase in the CD concentration in the solution. The results thus indicate the interaction of CD with 6-MP, and in this case the interaction has the positive impact with either the mass transport of 6-MP to the electrode surface or the 6-MP/CD adduct may get attached better with the AuNPs and the presence of highly conducting CD essentially enhances the electron transport process of 6-MP, thus enhances the electrochemical oxidation current of 6-MP (cf. Fig. 1D). Both the phenomena would contribute in overall enhancement of the peak current of 6-MP. In view of this, the analytical measurements have been proposed with enhanced sensitivity from the 6-MP in 0.01 µM CD containing solution. The voltammetric scans were recorded at varying concentration of 6-MP.and shown in Fig. 1E. The peak current of 6-MP enhances gradually with the increasing concentration of 6-MP in the solution. The calibration plot was constructed and shown in Fig. 1F, which has been fitted with linear equation with chi2 value as 0.998 and sum of square of all residuals as 4.68 × 10-15, and the sensitivity of the measurements is obtained as 3.86 µA/mM of 6-MP. In the present modified electrode, the detection limit of 6-MP is found to be 9.7 µM using AuNPs modified GCE as a working electrode. Apart from the AuNPs, some other modified electrodes are also investigated for the electrochemical detection of 6-MP in some recent reports, M. Keyvanfard et al. have used multiwall carbon nano tubes (CNT) paste electrode for the detection of 6-MP from the solution [23], the CNT infusion in the carbon paste made the electrode suitable for the analytical determination of 6-MP. In another work, CNT-TiO2 was used for the determination of 6-MP, provided high sensitive analytical method [2,24].

The repeatability of the electrochemical measurements over the same modified electrode and the variation of the electrochemical performance across the modified electrode fabricated using the same procedure has been presented in Figure S2 of the supporting information. The relative standard deviation of the measured current across different scans remained within 2 % (cf. Table S1).

In order to evaluate the nature of the charge transport in presence of CD, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy experiments were carried out in 6-MP solution with and without the presence of CD, the results are shown in Figure S3 of the supporting information. Experiments are recorded at the peak potential of the respective electrochemical processes. Typical semi-circular characteristics of the Nyquist plot is observed. The series resistance value, without and with CD in the test solution was obtained as 25 and 24 Ω respective. Interestingly it is observed that with the inclusion of CD in the test solution the charge transfer resistance is decreased from 1.4 × 105 Ω to 3.2 × 104 Ω. Such significant decrease of the charge transfer resistance thus indicates the improvement of the electrochemical process of 6-MP using CD in the test solution.

In order to further understand the interaction pattern between the 6-MP and CD, the particle size measurement is carried out using dynamic light scattering measurements and the result is shown in Fig. 2 A. The hydrated particle size of the CDs is obtained as 5.8 nm. The particle size measurements were also carried out from TEM measurements and shown in Fig. 2B. The particle size from the TEM has been indicated to be at around 2.5 nm. The hydration and coagulation may be the reason for the difference in size between the two measurements.

The zeta potential of 6-MP solution at neutral pH media is obtained as −14 mV and that of CD is −5mV, corresponding plots are shown in Fig. 2C. With the addition of CD in the 6-MP containing solution the zeta potential value of 6-MP is shifted towards more positive potential, the observation thus indicates the interaction and the adduct formation between 6-MP and CD. During the electrochemical reaction, therefore the adduct of the 6-MP and CD essentially undergo preferential transfer to the electrode surface and resulted higher oxidation current.

In order to substantiate the interaction pattern between the 6-MP and CD, the FTIR experiments are carried out, corresponding plots are shown in Fig. 2D. The experiments are recorded directly in the solution containing 6-MP with subsequent addition of CD in to it. The peaks at ∼ 3400 and ∼ 1630 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristics NH stretching and NH bending respectively for the functional groups present in 6-MP. The broad band in the 2500 cm−1 region is due to the SH stretching of the thiol functional group present in 6-MP. The CD solution was added subsequently in the 6-MP containing solution, it has been observed that the intensity of the band at ∼ 3400 cm−1 has been decreased in presence of CD and finally the peak is disappeared at higher CD concentration. The NH bending band of 6-MP at 1600 cm−1 also reduced with the addition of 6-MP in the solution. The peak due to thiol functional group also disappeared with the addition of CD in the solution. The observation indicates the interaction and complex formation between the 6-MP and CD [55–57]. One prominent FTIR peak appeared with the addition of CD at 2300 cm−1, the peak is essentially generated due to the complex between the 6-MP with CD. The peak may be a damped SH stretching peak in the complex. The FTIR measurements thus indicates that 6-MP engages most of its functional groups while making complex with CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic investigation of 6-MP and its interaction with CD
ce:section.ce:para:
Spectroscopic scans were also recorded to evaluate the interaction between 6-MP and CD, corresponding pots are shown in Fig. 3 C, D & E. The absorption spectra of CD alone are shown in Fig. 3C, the absorption peak is observed at 342 nm and the peak height is increased with the addition of CD in the test solution. The UV–vis absorbance spectroscopic scans were also conducted for CD with continued addition of 6-MP in the solution. As seen from the Fig. 3D, the absorption peaks corresponding to the 6-MP continued to emerge strong and the peak increased at the peak position of 6-MP. In order to obtain the formation constant of the complex between the 6-MP and CD, the double reciprocal plots are constructed and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. 3E following the equation (1).
(1)
A 0
A - A 0
=
ε G
ε H - G - ε G
+
ε G
ε H - G - ε G
×
1
Kb [ 6 - M P ]
where, A0 and A are the absorption of CD in absence and presence of 6-MP respectively, where A0 and A are measured at 340 nm. ƐG and ƐG-H represents the molar extinction coefficients of the CD and 6-MP-CD complex, respectively. The double reciprocal plot generated the linear correlation and from the ratio of the intercept to the slope of the linear correlation the formation constant of the 6-MP and CD complex is obtained as 4.8 × 105 M−1. Thus, the formation constant is high enough to be called as strong complex formation between 6-MP and CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectroscopy for interaction between 6-MP and CD
ce:section.ce:para:
CD being a fluorescing particle, the process of fluorescence quenching upon interaction with the drug molecule is used to characterize the drug-CD interaction in the solution. The solution of CD was excited at 350 nm and fluorescence spectrum was recorded with subsequent addition of 6-MP, corresponding results are shown in Fig. S4(A) of the supporting information. The fluorescence peak of CD is obtained at 445 nm. While due to the interaction between the CD with 6-MP the fluorescence intensity of CD is observed to be quenched. The fluorescence quenching experiments were carried out at varied concentrations of 6-MP and the Stern-Volmer plot is constructed and shown in Fig. S4(B).
(2)
F 0
/ F - 1 = K
Q
=
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]
where K is the Stern-Vomer constant, Kq is the quenching constant and τ0 is the lifetime of the CD without any addition. The data points were fitted with the Stern-Volmer fitting, which provided the Stern-Volmer constant K, of 26,744 LM−1 [5], The lifetime, τ0 of CD, is observed from the TCSPC measurements as 14.2 ns at neutral pH of the present experiments. Therefore, the quenching constant kq is obtained as 1.9 × 1012 M−1s−1. For a diffusion limiting process the quenching constant is observed to be in range of 1 × 1010 M−1s−1. The observed values are significantly higher than the diffusion-controlled limit of the quenching rate[58–60]. The significantly higher quenching rate indicates that the 6-MP and CD are not only interacting through the diffusion controlled dynamic process, there also exists a different strong chemical interaction mechanism through which the two species forms the complex. This has been evident from the reasonably strong binding constants between 6-MP and CD.

The interaction between the 6-MP and dsDNA has been investigated from the electrochemical and the UV–vis spectroscopic measurements. Electrochemical scans were recorded with the addition dsDNA in the solution, as seen from Fig. 4 A, the oxidation peak of 6-MP undergo positive shifting with the addition of dsDNA in the solution. The shift in the peak potentials are correlated with the concentration of dsDNA in the solution and the correlation generated the straight-line fitting (cf. Fig. 4B), which has been correlated with the equation (3).
(3)
(
E p
)
6 M P - d s D N A
-
(
E p
)
6 M P
⇔ -
0.059
n
log
k f
-
0.059 p
n
log
C
dsDNA
where
(
E p
)
6 M P - d s D N A
is the peak potential of 6-MP in presence of dsDNA,
(
E p
)
6 M P
is the peak potential of 6-MP without the presence of dsDNA.
k f
is the formation constant and the
C
dsDNA
is the concentration of dsDNA, p is the stoichiometry of the complex and n is the number of electrons transferred during the electrochemical oxidation process of 6-MP. From the analysis the low formation constant value of 35.5 M−1 has been obtained for 6-MP and dsDNA, which indicates the weak complexation between the two.

UV–vis spectroscopic experiments were also carried out to evaluate the interaction between the 6MP with dsDNA, where the dsDNA was added successively in the 6-MP containing solution, corresponding results are shown in Fig. 4C. The peak for 6-MP remained unchanged with the addition of dsDNA in the solution. The peak due to dsDNA is observed at 265 nm, and the absorbance increases with subsequent addition of dsDNA in the solution. No modification of either of the absorption peaks are observed due their interaction. It is very well known fact that intercalation of even small molecules in dsDNA strands leads to bathochromic shift as well as hypochromism [61] in its absorbance spectrum. Since bathochromic shift or hypochromism was not observed on spectroscopic interaction of 6-MP with dsDNA, the intercalation mode of binding of 6-MP with dsDNA can be ruled out in current study [62]. Since there is no clear isobestic point in the 6-MP-dsDNA complex spectra, it is an indicator that the interaction may involve more than one binding mode or 1:1 stoichiometry of 6-MP: dsDNA is not maintained throughout the process of interaction [63].

Interaction of 6-MP with dsDNA was monitored by AFM as imaging tool. 6-MP was allowed to interact with 100 μM dsDNA with increasing concentrations of 6-MP from 0.1 mM, 0.5 mM, 1 mM, 5 mM and by keeping the incubation time of interaction between drug and dsDNA constant at 30 min. An aliquot of 100 μL of this solution was drop casted on to the mica sheet and dried inside the biosafety cabinet. Corresponding AFM scans are shown in Fig. 5 . On interaction with 6-MP, the dsDNA loses its native structure and gets flattened and forms coagulate on mica sheets. As the concentration of 6-MP increases, the degree of coagulation and the damage caused to the native structure of dsDNA upon interaction with 6-MP enhances. The complete deformation of the dsDNA helical structure at the highest concentration of the drug at 5 mM. Fig. 5 shows a stepwise increase in the concentration of 6-MP showing increasing degree of dsDNA damage. To compare the modifications caused to dsDNA on interaction with 6-MP, the AFM images of pure untreated dsDNA was captured as a blank or negative control which shows its native strand structure (Fig. 5B). The present investigation with the AFM measurements therefore indicates that the morphology of the dsDNA modifies significantly in presence of 6-MP in the incubation solution. The resulted morphological variation in presence of 6-MP further confirms the interaction between the 6-MP and dsDNA. The swelling of the dsDNA strands indicates the ingress of foreign substrates inside the DNA strands resulting in the modification of the strand morphology. In a previously reported literature the molecular docking study reveals the interaction between the 6-MP and dsDNA, the investigation further reported about the groove binding as the mechanism of interaction [62]. In the groove binding process the 6-MP molecules are entangled inside the dsDNA strands. The indications about entanglement of 6-MP and the resulted swelling of the dsDNA strands has been supported from the AFM investigations.

Therefore, present investigation evaluates the electrochemical oxidation of 6-MP. CD has improved the electrochemical response of 6-MP by improving the mass transfer process and also through the enhancing the charge transfer process while attaching over the electrode surface. Electrochemical and spectrophotometric investigations further indicate that CD makes complex with 6-MP and the data generated may be useful in utilisation of CD as a carrier for the 6-MP drug. The electrochemical investigation revealed that 6-MP interacts weakly with dsDNA, which has been revealed from the low formation constant value of 35.5 M−1. The swelling and precipitation pattern of dsDNA has been observed in presence of 6-MP in the AFM scans which supports the groove binding interactions.

Present work reports the electrochemical property of 6-MP in the line to develop an analytical method and to investigate the interaction of 6-MP with dsDNA and HSA. The gold nano particle modified glassy carbon electrode has improved the electrochemical response of 6-MP. The detection limit of 6-MP with the developed sensor has been observed as 9.7 µM using electrochemical method. Analytical method for 6-MP also has been developed using UV–vis spectroscopic measurements and a detection limit of 4.1 µM has been obtained. The interaction between 6-MP and CD are investigated, the information revealed that CD forms strong complex with 6-MPand the formation constant are obtained as 5.25 × 104 M−1. Measurements from zeta potential supports electrochemical and spectroscopic investigations about the interaction between 6-MP and dsDNA. The strong interaction has also been evident from the fluorescence quenching experiments. The interaction between the anticancer drug has been investigated with dsDNA using electrochemical, spectroscopic and AFM measurements. Weak complexation between the 6-MP and dsDNA has been revealed from the electrochemical measurements. Swelling of the dsDNA strands has been observed from the AFM scans due to the interaction with 6-MP.
Priyanka R. Ipte: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Sudipa Manna: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ashis Kumar Satpati: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors thank Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC) for fully funding of the project. Authors also thank Dr. A. K. Tyagi, Director, Chemistry Group and Dr. C. N. Patra, Head, Analytical Chemistry Division for the encouragements and supports in the project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108159.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Clinical pharmacology and pharmacogenetics of thiopurines
Eur. J. Clin. Pharmacol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sahasranaman, D. Howard, S. Roy, Clinical pharmacology and pharmacogenetics of thiopurines, European journal of clinical pharmacology, 64 (2008) 753-767.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of 6-mercaptopurine in the presence of uric acid using modified multiwall carbon nanotubes-TiO2 as a voltammetric sensor
Drug Test. Anal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.A. Ensafi, H. Karimi‐Maleh, Determination of 6‐mercaptopurine in the presence of uric acid using modified multiwall carbon nanotubes‐TiO2 as a voltammetric sensor, Drug testing and analysis, 4 (2012) 970-977.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in carbon nanomaterials-based electrochemical sensors for food azo dyes detection
